title: Remedy and Reaction: The Peculiar American Struggle over Health Care Reform By Paul Starr Yale University Press 2011. 324 pages. $28.50 cloth, $17 paper
abstract: I am genuinely impressed with Remedy and Reaction as an ­historical accounting of the American health care system. Starr offers an ­historically rich explanation of the political difficulties encountered by those attempting to reform the system as well as insight into the world of ­compromise and missed opportunities that frame our current health care system. As I began reading Remedy and Reaction, I expected that the question of why universal access to health care does not exist in the United States would frame the book. While the question itself is not the central focus of the book, it does, however, frame the argument. Addressing the question of universal health care, Starr offers two explanations grounded in two distinct historical periods. It is the second explanation that is the premise of the book. The first period covers approximately the first four decades of the 20th century. In this period, Starr states that “the relative weakness of unions and socialists in the United States meant that there was no corresponding political basis for a government health insurance program when reformers first tried to pass it in the Progressive era.”(73) The second historical period covers the emergence of job-based health insurance coverage as well as federal passage of government-sponsored health care programs for specific segments of the population. It is the health policy reform efforts of this historical period that are the focus of the book.
